_notes: Drives the KB section of the Agents & KB page. Each article maps to a known incident pattern. domain should match a service domain in applications.json. tags drive search. body is the runbook summary shown in the list view.

articles.title: GitLab CI Runner OOM — Diagnosis & Self-Heal
articles.body: GitLab CI runner OOM kills most commonly caused by undersized JVM heap (-Xmx 2g) combined with Nexus disk pressure causing dependency resolution retries. Fix: scale runner pool via K8s HPA + increase -Xmx to 4g + Nexus SNAPSHOT cleanup. ZeroOps auto-heals via pattern recognition — MTTR 11 min.

sections.title: Overview & Engineering Impact
sections.content: GitLab CI runners use JVM-based Gradle/Maven build execution. The default -Xmx 2g heap is insufficient for large monorepo builds when Nexus Artifactory is under disk pressure. Dependency resolution retries multiply heap consumption per build. At peak, a single large monorepo build can consume 3.8g of heap. When 8 runners are all executing large builds simultaneously, OOM kills cascade. 2,400 R&D engineers are blocked within 8 minutes of the first OOM kill.

sections.title: Diagnosis Steps
sections.content:
1. Check K8s events: kubectl get events -n gitlab-runners | grep OOMKilled
2. Verify Nexus disk: check /data partition — if >85% this is the upstream cause
3. Review runner logs: JVM heap usage and dependency fetch latency
4. Confirm: ZeroOps Alert Correlator will have already clustered the events

sections.title: Immediate Relief — Scale Runner Pool
sections.content:
kubectl patch hpa gitlab-runner-hpa -p '{"spec":{"minReplicas":24}}'
This unblocks the pipeline queue while the root cause (Nexus disk) is addressed.

sections.title: Permanent Fix — JVM Resize + Nexus Cleanup
sections.content:
1. Patch ConfigMap: JAVA_OPTS=-Xmx 4g -Xms 2g
2. Rolling restart runners: kubectl rollout restart deployment/gitlab-runner
3. Execute NEXUS-CLEANUP runbook via OneEngine — targets SNAPSHOTs >30 days
4. Re-enable retention policy: 30-day limit / 5 versions per component

sections.title: Prevention
sections.content: Nexus SNAPSHOT retention policy must be treated as safety-critical. Disabling during maintenance requires a mandatory re-enable checklist step. ZeroOps Capacity Planner alert threshold: 70% disk. Alert at 70% gives 6h lead time before impact.

articles.title: Nexus Artifactory Disk — Retention & Cleanup
articles.body: Nexus /data partition fills when SNAPSHOT artifact retention policy is disabled or misconfigured. Recovery: archive SNAPSHOTs >30 days, keep last 5 per component. Always re-enable retention policy after maintenance windows. Add 70% disk alert threshold. OneEngine NEXUS-CLEANUP runbook recovers 400GB+ in 8 min.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Nexus /nexus-data stores all build artifacts for the R&D engineering platform. At current build cadence (~12,000 builds/day), SNAPSHOT artifacts accumulate at ~140GB/week when retention policy is inactive. At 91% disk utilisation, artifact fetch I/O degrades to 29s+ per dependency — directly causing GitLab runner JVM heap exhaustion.

sections.title: Recovery — OneEngine NEXUS-CLEANUP
sections.content:
Trigger via ZeroOps: OneEngine runbook NEXUS-CLEANUP
Manual: POST /service/rest/v1/script/cleanup/run via Nexus REST API
Targets: SNAPSHOTs older than 30 days across all repositories
Expected reclaim: 400-600GB depending on accumulation period
ETA: 6-10 minutes

sections.title: Retention Policy Configuration
sections.content:
Navigate to: Nexus Admin → Repository → Cleanup Policies
Create policy: Age = 30 days, Keep last N = 5 versions
Assign to all SNAPSHOT repositories
Verify: policy shows as Active after maintenance windows

articles.title: SAP S/4HANA Work Process Saturation — Month-End
articles.body: SAP dialog WP saturation during month-end caused by batch jobs (FI_POST_CLOSE, GL_RECON) running in dialog WP class concurrently with user sessions. Fix: defer batch via SM37, increase dialog WPs via RZ10. Dual approval required: BASIS admin (deferral) + BASIS admin (WP config). Azure E32s_v5 → E48s_v5 resize may be needed for repeated saturation.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: SAP S/4HANA Finance runs on Azure VM Standard_E32s_v5 (32 vCPU, 256GB RAM). During month-end, batch jobs compete with 340 finance users in the same dialog WP class. The E32s_v5 provides 100 dialog WPs — at month-end peak, all 100 can be consumed within 20 minutes of batch start. The CFO office tracks period-end close completion directly.

sections.title: Diagnosis Steps
sections.content:
1. SM50: Check WP utilisation — if >80%, saturation risk confirmed
2. SM37: Identify running batch jobs and their WP class
3. SM21: Check for ABAP dumps (rdisp/max_wprun_time exceeded)
4. Azure Monitor: Check VM CPU — if >85%, scale-up may be required

sections.title: Immediate Relief — Defer Batch Jobs
sections.content:
SM37 → Select FI_POST_CLOSE and GL_RECON → Reschedule to +20 minutes
This frees ~35% WP capacity immediately
SOX compliance: GL_RECON deferral up to 2h is within approved window
Requires: BASIS admin approval before execution

sections.title: Increase WP Allocation (RZ10)
sections.content:
RZ10 → Instance profile → rdisp/wp_no_dia
Increase from 100 to 130
No SAP restart required for this parameter
Requires: BASIS admin approval and change record (CHG)

articles.title: Entra ID Conditional Access — Rollback Procedure
articles.body: CA policy deployment without B2B guest account testing is the most common cause of Entra MFA latency spikes. Rollback procedure: retrieve previous policy version from Entra audit log, validate diff, apply via Entra admin API. Latency restores within 5 minutes of rollback. Always test CA policies in a sandbox tenant against B2B guest accounts before production deployment.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Entra Conditional Access policies affect all users including B2B guest accounts. A misconfigured policy can trigger recursive evaluation loops on legacy guest accounts, causing token issuance latency to spike from 280ms to 4s+. This impacts all services using Entra SSO — Teams, GitLab, Jira, and SAP SSO.

sections.title: Rollback Procedure
sections.content:
1. Entra Admin Center → Conditional Access → Audit log — identify last policy change
2. Export previous policy version JSON
3. Validate diff against current policy — identify the recursive trigger
4. Apply rollback via Microsoft Graph API: PATCH /identity/conditionalAccess/policies/{id}
5. Monitor: token issuance latency should restore to <300ms within 5 minutes

articles.title: ServiceNow CMDB Drift — Detection & Remediation
articles.body: CMDB CI drift corrupts ZeroOps blast radius calculations and RCA topology. Weekly automated scan via Change Validator detects drift early. Three categories: Azure VM spec changes, Ericsson DC server config, org reorg owner changes. Always validate corrections with CMDB owner before bulk apply via Update Sets.

sections.title: Why CMDB Accuracy Matters for ZeroOps
sections.content: ZeroOps uses the CMDB topology graph for: (1) blast radius calculation during RCA, (2) SME identification for swarm assembly, (3) change impact assessment in Change Validator. Stale CI records directly affect the quality of autonomous decisions. A CI showing the wrong owner will page the wrong engineer. A CI showing the wrong hosting will miscalculate blast radius.

sections.title: Detection
sections.content:
ZeroOps Change Validator runs a weekly CMDB drift scan comparing:
- Azure VM specs (vCPU, RAM, SKU) against Azure ARM API
- Ericsson DC server configuration against DC management API
- CI ownership against HR system (org changes not propagated)
Drift report generated as ServiceNow task assigned to CMDB team.

articles.title: ZeroOps AIOps Remediation Playbook — Ericsson IT
articles.body: Master autonomous remediation playbook for Ericsson IT Operations. LOW risk (<25): Sentinel auto-executes via OneEngine. MEDIUM risk (25-70): Guardian HiTL — NOC or system owner approval. HIGH risk (>70): Advisor — senior engineer sign-off. SAP and security policy changes always require explicit approval regardless of risk score.

sections.title: Three-Pillar Decision Model
sections.content:
SENTINEL (risk <25): ZeroOps acts autonomously. Reversible action, no user impact, high historical success rate. No human notification unless action fails.

GUARDIAN (risk 25-70): ZeroOps prepares the complete RCA, blast radius, and remediation plan. One human approval gate. Human is accountable for the decision; ZeroOps executes.

ADVISOR (risk >70 or SAP/Security): ZeroOps surfaces the richest diagnostic brief possible. Senior engineer makes the call and executes with ZeroOps guidance.

sections.title: Ericsson-Specific Governance Rules
sections.content:
1. SAP production changes: always Guardian or Advisor regardless of calculated risk score
2. Security policy changes (Entra CA, CrowdStrike, Zscaler): always Advisor
3. Ericsson DC physical actions: DC team notification required
4. Azure VM resize: CHG record required, CAB approval for >E16s
5. Database schema changes: DBA team approval always required
